daily_brief: The United States continued its tenth consecutive night of strikes against Iran as the conflict over the Strait of Hormuz intensified, with Iran claiming retaliatory attacks on U.S. military assets in Bahrain and Kuwait [BBC, Financial Times, Globe and Mail]. President Trump warned of further retaliation following American military casualties, while Iran declared it was now fighting a 'full-scale war' with the U.S. [BBC]. Adding to regional instability, Yemen's Houthis announced a maritime embargo against Saudi Arabia, threatening to close the Bab al-Mandab Strait to Saudi vessels [BBC]. In North America, Trump imposed 50% tariffs on Canadian goods using an untested Depression-era law, targeting dairy, alcohol, and hundreds of other products in retaliation for Ottawa's counter-tariffs [BBC, Globe and Mail]. Britain saw its seventh prime minister in a decade as Andy Burnham entered Downing Street, promising measures to address the cost-of-living crisis and pledging to remove VAT on household electricity [Le Monde, Financial Times, RFI]. Meanwhile, Spain celebrated its World Cup victory with a royal welcome and parade through Madrid attended by nearly two million fans [BBC, Le Monde].

top_stories.headline: U.S. Launches Tenth Night of Strikes on Iran as Both Sides Escalate Toward Full-Scale War
bullets: U.S. Central Command conducted its tenth consecutive night of strikes targeting Iranian military command centers, missile and drone launch sites, maritime capabilities, and air defense systems
Iran's Revolutionary Guards claimed retaliatory strikes on U.S. military targets in Bahrain and Kuwait, including air defense systems and an Amazon data center in Bahrain
Iran announced it stopped two oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz and struck two additional tankers, while President Pezeshkian declared Iran was now fighting a 'full-scale war' with the U.S.
Nearly 100 U.S. troops have been injured in two weeks since fighting resumed; diplomatic messages continue through intermediaries despite the collapsed ceasefire

sources.name: BBC World
sources.framing_note: Emphasized Trump's vow to punish Iran for U.S. soldier deaths and provided detailed account of Iranian counterclaims

sources.name: Le Monde
sources.framing_note: Headline focused on Trump's warning about American soldier deaths

sources.name: Financial Times
sources.framing_note: Highlighted U.S. troop injury count as primary angle

sources.name: Globe and Mail
sources.framing_note: Emphasized diplomatic glimmers of hope alongside military escalation

sources.name: The Hindu
sources.framing_note: Provided comprehensive live coverage of Iranian Guard claims

top_stories.headline: Trump Imposes 50% Tariffs on Canadian Goods Using Untested Depression-Era Law
bullets: President Trump signed proclamations invoking Section 338 of the 1930 Tariff Act—never previously used—to impose 50% tariffs on nearly $20 billion worth of Canadian goods
Targeted products include dairy, alcohol, hockey sticks, cement, electronic equipment, and Christmas ornaments; energy, potash, critical minerals, and fish are exempt
Tariffs take effect August 19 and will not include USMCA exemptions, representing a major escalation after the Supreme Court ruled many of Trump's emergency tariffs were illegally enacted
Prime Minister Mark Carney said Canada stands ready to 'intensify' trade talks; Canada had previously maintained 25% retaliatory tariffs on U.S. cars, steel, and aluminum

sources.name: BBC World
sources.framing_note: Focused on legal context and the untested nature of the tariff law used

sources.name: Globe and Mail
sources.framing_note: Emphasized the escalation narrative and detailed product categories affected; noted Canada was rare in retaliating against earlier tariffs

top_stories.headline: Yemen's Houthis Announce Maritime Embargo Against Saudi Arabia, Threatening Red Sea Shipping
bullets: Iran-backed Houthis declared an immediate maritime embargo on Saudi Arabia, claiming they would close the Bab al-Mandab Strait to Saudi vessels
The move is in retaliation for what Houthis described as a Saudi blockade of ports and airports in Houthi-controlled northwestern Yemen
Saudi Arabia condemned the allegations and vowed to take all necessary measures to protect its ships under international law
The Red Sea has become crucial for Saudi oil shipments as the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed due to the U.S.-Iran conflict

sources.name: BBC World
sources.framing_note: Provided context on the dormant Saudi-Houthi conflict and previous escalations

sources.name: Globe and Mail
sources.framing_note: Briefly noted the Houthi announcement as adding to energy market threats

sources.name: The Hindu
sources.framing_note: Connected the development to India's NSA visit to Saudi Arabia to discuss energy security and maritime corridors

top_stories.headline: Andy Burnham Becomes Britain's Seventh Prime Minister in a Decade, Announces Cost-of-Living Measures
bullets: Andy Burnham formally became Prime Minister after King Charles III asked him to form a government at Buckingham Palace following Keir Starmer's resignation
Burnham appointed former Defense Minister John Healey as Chancellor of the Exchequer and announced plans to remove VAT on household electricity bills
The new PM pledged a 10-year plan for Britain, promising to end rough sleeping, improve living standards, and provide a 'circuit breaker' for political and economic instability
Burnham is currently the most popular leader among Britain's main political parties and won Labour leadership unopposed

sources.name: Le Monde
sources.framing_note: Focused on cabinet appointments, particularly Healey as Chancellor

sources.name: Financial Times
sources.framing_note: Led with the VAT removal policy as headline news

sources.name: RFI English
sources.framing_note: Emphasized the 'seventh PM in a decade' instability narrative

sources.name: Globe and Mail
sources.framing_note: Detailed Burnham's policy agenda and political challenges from Reform UK

top_stories.headline: Russian Strikes Kill Ten on Cargo Ship Off Odesa as Black Sea Attacks Intensify
bullets: A Guinea-Bissau-flagged cargo ship was struck by Russian cruise missiles off Odesa on Sunday, killing nine crew members and one Ukrainian pilot
Four Indian nationals were among the dead; India's foreign ministry condemned the endangerment of crew members and impediment of navigation freedom
Twenty-eight people have died in Russian strikes on the Odesa region in July alone, with attacks on cargo ships increasing significantly
Maritime exports account for over two-thirds of Ukrainian agricultural exports, worth roughly $9 billion, making the corridor essential for Kyiv's revenue

sources.name: BBC Europe
sources.framing_note: Comprehensive coverage emphasizing the humanitarian toll and economic implications for Ukraine's export corridor

additional_stories.headline: Tate brothers appear in Miami federal court to fight UK extradition on 38 charges including rape and sex trafficking
Russian warship conducts live-fire artillery exercise 40 nautical miles off Plymouth coast while monitored by Royal Navy
Sinaloa Cartel co-founder Ismael 'El Mayo' Zambada sentenced to life in U.S. prison and ordered to pay $15 billion
Spain celebrates World Cup victory with royal welcome and Madrid parade attended by nearly two million fans
France and Germany push for stronger EU response to China's trade surplus and industrial overcapacity
Ukraine faces fifth day of protests demanding return of dismissed defense minister and removal of commander-in-chief
Philippines summons Chinese Ambassador after South China Sea encounter leaves Navy personnel injured
Israel constructing 14-mile earthen barrier inside Gaza, entrenching division of the territory
Lebanon's President Aoun meets Trump in Washington to push for Israeli troop withdrawal from southern Lebanon
ASEAN foreign ministers gather in Manila amid warnings bloc risks being 'pulled apart' by external pressures
